content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Ensemble Prompting: Reducing Social Bias in LLM Outputs via Prompt Diversification",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often reproduce or amplify social biases and stereotypes in their generated text. Existing prompting techniques insufficiently mitigate these biases, especially for complex or subtle contexts.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly integrated into societal and decision-making contexts, their unbiased operation becomes critical. Current bias mitigation mostly relies on post-hoc filtering or single-shot prompt engineering, which can be brittle or incomplete. Effective, scalable, and model-agnostic prompting methods to reduce social biases are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual Ensemble Prompting (CEP), wherein, for each input, the prompt is automatically expanded into a diverse set of counterfactual variants by systematically altering sociocultural variables (e.g., gender, ethnicity, profession) using a controlled template engine. The model generates outputs for each variant, and the final response is synthesized using a bias-aware aggregation function (e.g., majority vote or contrastive re-ranking) that penalizes stereotypical or biased patterns. The mechanism requires no model retraining and can be applied to any LLM.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CEP on the CrowS-Pairs and StereoSet bias benchmarks, comparing against standard prompts and self-debiasing prompt strategies. Metrics will include bias score reduction, semantic consistency, and output fluency. The primary baseline is the self-debiasing prompt technique by Schick et al. (2021). A blinded human evaluation will assess stereotype reduction without loss of informativeness."
}
